reviews.why: Different objects/metrics. (A) alcohol_sales_mix tracks the share of sales coming from the alcohol category (beverages), with all five quotes about "alcohol mix," "alcohol trade down," "alcohol component/category." (B) bar_sales_mix tracks the share of sales coming from the bar as a venue/concept ("bar incident rates," "Bar mix is about 23%," "important part of the concept"). The bar is a physical sales channel that includes food and non-alcoholic items, not just alcohol; and alcohol is sold beyond the bar (dining tables). Scope differs: A = product/category mix, B = venue/channel mix. Mechanism differs: A moves with consumer alcohol spending (trade-down), B moves with bar traffic/incident rates. Token overlap (mix, sales) is superficial. Keep separate.

Scope differs. "appetizer_attachment_rate" is specifically the rate at which appetizers are added to orders ("higher appetizer attachment rates", "year-over-year growth in appetizer attachment rates and improved entree ordering patterns"). The bare "attachment_rate" quote ("regarding, say, attachments rates...we're still above 2019 levels") is generic add-on attach rate with no product narrowing — it could cover appetizers, but also desserts, beverages, alcohol, or any add-on category. Object/metric (an attach rate) overlaps, but the appetizer driver is a strict sub-scope of the general one, not the same scope. Merging would wrongly fold a product-specific driver into a broad one (over-merge risk). Note also that side b carries same_as_variants:["guest_incident_rate"], suggesting its identity is already ambiguous/contested. Scope test fails → DIFFERENT. Keep separate.

Same metric TYPE (average unit volume = sales per restaurant location) but DIFFERENT SCOPE, so the 3-part test fails on scope. Side a's evidence is overwhelmingly the flagship "Cheesecake Factory" brand with AUVs of ~$12.0-12.8M ("average unit volumes of over $12 million for domestic cheesecake factory restaurants"; "Annualized unit volumes at the Cheesecake Factory reached 12.5 million"). Side b is explicitly the separate Flower Child concept ("dig in a little more on the flower child... potential average volumes for a fully mature box"; "$4.4 million average unit volume"). These track two distinct restaurant brands with materially different unit economics ($12M vs $4.4M). The name flower_child_average_unit_volume exists precisely to keep Flower Child's AUV separate from the flagship AUV. Token overlap (average/unit/volume) is a metric-name coincidence, not a same-object match. Merging would conflate different brands' performance (over-merge). Keep separate.

Same metric type (average unit volume = average sales per restaurant location) and same mechanism, BUT different SCOPE. Side A "average_unit_volume" covers The Cheesecake Factory flagship brand (AUVs ~$12.1-12.8M; evidence quotes "Average sales per location for The Cheesecake Factory restaurants", "domestic cheesecake factory restaurants", "annualized unit volumes at the Cheesecake Factory"). Side B "north_italia_average_unit_volume" is scoped specifically to the North Italia concept, a separate restaurant brand CAKE operates (AUVs ~$7.7-8.0M; evidence quotes "for North Italia restaurants", "North Italia first quarter comparable sales... resulting in annualized AUVs of $7.7 million"). The dollar figures confirm two distinct populations ($12M+ vs ~$8M). Brand-level driver scope must stay separate per the own-driver / brand-scope rule. Token overlap (average/unit/volume) is the metric-type name only; the brand qualifier "north_italia" changes the scope. Scope fails → DIFFERENT, keep separate.
Keep separate — these are sibling slices of the sales mix, not the same driver.

Object differs: "beef_cost" is the broad beef commodity category ("higher beef costs"), while "ground_beef_cost" is one specific subset — ground beef — which the company explicitly treats as a distinct, harder-to-contract item ("ground beef, harder to get a long-term contract there"). This is a parent/subset relationship, not the same object, so test #1 (same object/metric) fails. Mechanism (input commodity cost) is similar, but merging would erase the company's own distinction between general beef and ground beef's separate supply/contract dynamics. Keep separate.

These are unrelated drivers that only share the token "market". Object/metric: A measures the company's share of the casual dining restaurant industry (comparable sales/traffic outperformance vs peers); B describes broad stock price/volume swings in equity markets. Scope: A is restaurant-industry competitive position; B is general financial-market/stock-price behavior. Mechanism: A = capturing customers from competing restaurants; B = market-wide volatility unrelated to operating performance (a risk-factor boilerplate in 10-K filings). The evidence quotes confirm A is about "outperformance versus the broader casual dining industry" while B is about "equity markets...price and volume fluctuations...unrelated to an individual company's operating performance." All three tests fail. Keep separate.

Token overlap ("consumer", "spending") suggested this pair, but the evidence shows different scope and mechanism.  SCOPE differs: - A (consumer_discretionary_spending): the WHOLE consumer base's discretionary spending — broad, economy-wide. Quotes: "global and domestic economic conditions have on consumer discretionary spending," "Any factor affecting consumer discretionary spending may influence customer traffic." - B (high_income_consumer_spending): specifically the HIGH-INCOME cohort ("income of 100 plus K," "top end of consumers"). Narrow slice of the population.  MECHANISM differs: - A is a general macro headwind: weak economy → less discretionary spend → lower restaurant traffic/check. Direction = uniformly negative for CAKE. - B is a cohort-specific, even partly INVERSE mechanism: when high earners feel pressure they "typically move down off of the steakhouses and the expensive independents" — so CAKE may BENEFIT ("we're likely to actually benefit some from that"). The 2025 quote argues high-income jobs aren't disappearing, so this cohort's spend is resilient.  OBJECT/METRIC: A measures aggregate discretionary spending across all consumers; B measures the spending behavior of one income tier specifically.  A high-income-resilience driver (can be a tailwind) is not the same object as a broad discretionary-spending macro risk (a headwind). At least two of the three tests (scope, mechanism) fail. Keep separate.

Token overlap (consumer, spending) flagged these, but the evidence shows different scope and mechanism. A = general consumer spending across the whole consumer base: boilerplate risk language ("conditions that impact consumer confidence and spending") plus transcript talk of the overall consumer environment easing, trade-down, and macro pressure on consumers broadly. B is narrowly the high-income/affluent segment: "top end of consumers really does feel pressure... they typically move down off of the steakhouses and the expensive independents" and "income of 100 plus K... incomes aren't slowing down." Test: (1) same object — NO, A is all consumers vs B is the $100K+ subset (parent vs a carve-out segment); (2) same scope — NO, A is broad, B is restricted to affluent earners; (3) same mechanism — NO, B describes a segment-specific dynamic where high-end pressure can actually BENEFIT CAKE via trade-down, which can move opposite to the general consumer signal. CAKE itself treats the high-income cohort as distinct from the general consumer. Merging would collapse a meaningful distinction (over-merge). Keep separate.

Token overlap on "credit/tip" is misleading — these are two unrelated concepts that happen to share the word "tip credit."  OBJECT/METRIC — different: - A (fica_tip_tax_credit): a TAX CREDIT. Evidence is all about the SITCA program (a federal IRS tip-reporting compliance program) and qualifying for "tax credits." This is the FICA tip tax credit — money the company gets BACK from the IRS for FICA payroll taxes paid on employee tips. - B (tip_credit_wage): a WAGE RULE / labor-cost input. Evidence is all about "minimum wage rates," the "tip credit wage rate," and states "eliminating the tip credit wage." This is the labor-law rule that lets employers pay tipped workers a lower base wage (the "tip credit" against minimum wage).  SCOPE — different: - A = federal IRS tax matter (income tax credit, SITCA eligibility). - B = state/local minimum-wage and labor-cost matter ("certain states," "state and localities").  MECHANISM — different / opposite direction of impact: - A: losing the tax credit → higher taxes → hurts financial performance (tax line). - B: raising/eliminating the tip credit wage → higher wages paid to workers → wage-cost pressure (labor expense line).  The shared phrase "tip credit" refers to two entirely different things: a tax credit vs. a wage-offset against minimum wage. Same scope test (1), same object test (2), and same mechanism test (3) all FAIL. Keep separate.

Shared tokens are only the brand name "Flower Child," not a shared concept. A = a corporate M&A event (acquiring/integrating the Flower Child brand under the company umbrella). B = a brand-level operating metric (average weekly sales per store, $85,200, +6% YoY). Fails all three SAME tests: (1) different object/metric — an acquisition milestone vs a unit-economics sales figure; (2) different scope — company-level M&A vs per-store sales performance; (3) different mechanism — ownership/integration vs revenue generation per location. Keep separate.

Both mention the Flower Child brand (same company CAKE), so token overlap is real — but they describe two different things.  Side A "flower_child_acquisition": quote "bringing it fully under our umbrella for this year" = the M&A event of buying/fully owning the Flower Child brand. Object = ownership/acquisition. Mechanism = corporate deal.  Side B "flower_child_off_premise_mix": quote "a 55% off-premise mix" = an operating metric (share of sales that are takeout/delivery vs dine-in) at Flower Child. Object = sales-channel mix. Mechanism = consumer ordering behavior.  Test results: 1. Same object/metric? NO — acquisition event vs off-premise sales percentage. 2. Same scope? Partly (both = the Flower Child brand), but the subject differs. 3. Same mechanism? NO — corporate ownership deal vs day-to-day sales channel.  Two of three fail. Shared brand name is not enough to merge. Keep separate.

Token overlap fired on the shared BRAND name ("Flower Child"), not a shared driver. The two drivers describe different things: A = a corporate acquisition/ownership event ("bringing it fully under our umbrella"), an M&A/consolidation transaction; B = the recurring sales/revenue metric of the Flower Child chain ("sales increased 11.0% to $127.5M", "sales per operating week were $80,000"). Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS — a one-time ownership change is not a revenue figure. Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS — M&A consolidation vs. organic same-brand sales growth are distinct price drivers. Keep separate.

Both mention the same brand (Flower Child), but they track different objects/metrics with different mechanisms. "a" (flower_child_acquisition) is about the corporate M&A event of bringing Flower Child fully under CAKE's ownership ("bringing it fully under our umbrella") — a one-time acquisition/consolidation driver. "b" (flower_child_unit_count) is about the ongoing operational footprint and store-growth runway of the concept (38 locations now, ~700 potential, ~20% annual unit growth). Test 1 (same object/metric): FAIL — acquisition event vs. unit-count/store-expansion metric. Test 3 (same mechanism): FAIL — completing an ownership transaction vs. building new restaurant locations over time. Token overlap on "flower"/"child" is just the shared brand name, not a shared driver. Keep separate.

Token overlap (flower/child/unit) only. Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. (A) average_unit_volume = revenue per restaurant (AUV $4.4M, "average volumes for a fully mature box"). (B) unit_count = number of locations (38 Flower Child locations, ~700 potential, 20% annual unit growth). One measures dollars-per-store; the other counts stores. "unit volume" (sales/store) is a wholly different metric than "unit count" (store quantity), and B's own same_as_variant is flower_child_unit_development (location growth), confirming it tracks store count not sales. Fails object and mechanism tests; keep separate.

Different metric and scope despite same brand (Flower Child). Driver a = average weekly sales, a per-operating-week dollar rate / unit-economics figure (e.g. "$85,200, up more than 6%"). Driver b = total brand sales aggregated over a fiscal period (e.g. "$127.5 million for fiscal 2023", "$34.5 million for the first quarter"). Test 1 (same object/metric) fails: a weekly run-rate is not the same as total period revenue. Test 2 (same scope) fails: per-week normalized rate vs full quarter/year aggregate. Although b's transcript quotes sometimes mention "sales per operating week" as a side stat, b's defining metric is the headline total period sales, so the two drivers track genuinely different quantities. Merging would conflate an AUV-style rate with aggregate revenue. Keep separate.

Both are about the same brand (Flower Child, a CAKE-owned restaurant), but they describe two different things — so they fail the "same object/metric" and "same mechanism" tests.  Driver A "flower_child_integration": the 2023-05-10 quote "we continue down the integration path this year" is about INTEGRATING the acquired brand into the parent company (operational/M&A absorption work). Object = the integration process. Mechanism = absorbing the acquisition.  Driver B "flower_child_off_premise_mix": the 2024-05-08 quote "you're talking about a 55% off-premise mix" is a SALES-CHANNEL METRIC — the share of sales that are takeout/delivery (off-premise) vs dine-in. Object = a sales-mix percentage. Mechanism = how customers order.  Check the three SAME tests: 1. Same object/metric? NO — "integration progress" vs "off-premise sales %". Different things. 2. Same scope? Both scoped to the Flower Child brand at CAKE — this one overlaps, but it is the only one that matches. 3. Same mechanism? NO — absorbing an acquisition vs customer ordering channel.  Two of three are false. The token overlap ("flower", "child") only reflects the shared brand name, not a shared driver. Keep them separate.

Both drivers concern the same brand (Flower Child, CAKE's fast-casual concept), but they track different objects/mechanisms.  Object/metric test — FAILS: - "flower_child_integration" = the operational work of folding the acquired Flower Child concept into CAKE (the "integration path"). Evidence: "we continue down the integration path this year." This is about post-acquisition operational integration (systems, operations, scaling the concept). - "flower_child_same_store_sales" = a comparable-sales financial metric. All 13 evidence quotes report a comp-sales percentage (e.g., "comparable sales increased approximately 6%"). This is a recurring revenue performance metric, not integration work.  Mechanism test — FAILS: - Integration mechanism = absorbing/operationalizing an acquired brand (a one-off-to-multi-year transition project). - Same-store-sales mechanism = period-over-period demand/traffic/pricing at existing locations (an ongoing performance KPI).  The token overlap ("flower", "child") only reflects the shared brand name, not a shared driver. A brand can have many distinct drivers (integration progress, unit growth, comp sales, margins). These are two of them. Same brand ≠ same driver.  Merging would repeat the v2 over-merge error: collapsing an operational-progress driver into a revenue-metric driver just because they share a proper noun. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only reflects the shared brand (Flower Child restaurant), not the same driver. The two fail all three SAME tests: (1) Object/metric differs — "flower_child_margin" is about profit margin hitting a mid-teens target to make the brand a growth vehicle; "flower_child_rewards_program" is about a customer loyalty app with points. (2) Scope differs — one is a financial profitability measure for the chain, the other is a marketing/customer-engagement feature. (3) Mechanism differs — margin expansion via unit economics/cost vs. customer retention via app-based points. Brand match is not driver match. Keep separate.

Same brand (Flower Child), but they measure different objects via different mechanisms. (A) flower_child_margin = profitability target ("mid-teens" margin level to make it a growth vehicle) — a per-dollar economics metric. (B) flower_child_unit_count = number of restaurant locations (38 today, ~700 potential, ~20% annual unit growth) — a store-count/expansion metric. Object differs (profit margin vs store count), mechanism differs (operating efficiency vs new-location openings). Shared "flower"/"child" tokens are just the brand name; token overlap alone does not make them the same driver. The same_as_variants confirm distinct lanes: margin-side links to "restaurant_margin", count-side links to "unit_development". Fails criteria 1 (same object/metric) and 3 (same mechanism). Keep separate.

Same brand (Flower Child), but different metrics and mechanisms. A measures off-premise MIX — a channel ratio ("55% off-premise mix"), i.e. what share of sales are takeout/delivery vs dine-in. B measures total DOLLAR SALES/revenue ("$127.5M", "increased 10.2% to $34.5M", "per operating week were $80,000"). Object differs (a percentage channel split vs a revenue total), and mechanism differs (where customers order vs total brand demand). The two move independently — total sales can rise while off-premise mix falls. Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only reflects the shared brand, not the same driver. Test 1 (same object) and Test 3 (same mechanism) both fail → keep separate.

These share only the brand name "Flower Child" (a CAKE restaurant concept) — the token overlap that triggered the suggestion. They measure completely different things.  A = flower_child_rewards_program: an app-based customer loyalty/points program. Quote: "flower child is much more of a traditional rewards program it's an app based program that has points."  B = flower_child_unit_count: the number of physical restaurant locations and unit-growth runway. Quote: "Currently, we operate 38 Flower Child locations... potential for approximately 700 domestic locations... approximately 20% average annual unit growth."  Test against the three SAME criteria: 1. Same object/metric? NO — one is a rewards/loyalty program, the other is store count / unit expansion. 2. Same scope? NO — customer engagement vs. footprint/real-estate growth. 3. Same mechanism? NO — points-earning app vs. opening new locations.  All three fail. Brand match alone is not a duplicate; per the brand=own-driver rule the shared "Flower Child" name does not collapse distinct drivers. Keep them separate (no SAME_AS).

Only the brand name "Flower Child" is shared (the token overlap that triggered the suggestion). The metrics are different on all three tests. Object/metric: A is comparable/same-store sales growth (a percentage, e.g. "comparable sales increased approximately 6%"); B is the number of restaurant locations and unit growth (e.g. "we operate 38 Flower Child locations... 20% average annual unit growth"). Scope: A covers existing comparable stores' revenue; B covers the total store footprint/expansion. Mechanism: A reflects demand/pricing per existing store; B reflects physical store openings. These can move independently (units can grow while same-store sales fall). Keep separate.

Token overlap (sales/same/store) only. Two of the three tests fail. SCOPE: A is one specific brand only ("Flower Child comparable sales increased approximately 6%", "Fire Child comps"), a single fast-casual concept; B is the company-wide/blended comp outlook with no brand named ("do you expect to be in that 1% to 2% range on comp", "implied 2026 same-shore sales number"). MECHANISM: A is realized/historical actual results reported in 10-K/10-Q/transcripts; B is forward-looking guidance and management assumptions ("our comp assumptions", "high end of our guide", "do you expect"). Actuals-for-one-brand vs company-wide-forward-guidance are distinct drivers. Keep separate.

Same metric TYPE (restaurant unit count/growth) but DIFFERENT scope. Flower Child = one specific brand/concept (evidence: "we operate 38 Flower Child locations... ~20% average annual unit growth for this concept"). Fox Restaurant Concepts = the AGGREGATE "Other FRC portfolio" of brands (evidence: "10% to 15% average annual unit growth for the aggregate Other FRC portfolio"). Flower Child is a single brand within the FRC family, so one is a sub-scope and the other is the parent aggregate — different unit base, different growth target (20% vs 10-15%). Token overlap on "count"/"unit" is purely lexical. Fails Test 2 (scope) and Test 3 (mechanism). Keep separate.

These count two different restaurant brands. Side A's evidence is about "Flower Child locations" (38 locations, ~700 domestic potential). Side B's evidence is about "North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants" — a separate concept/brand owned by the same parent (CAKE = Cheesecake Factory). Same object test FAILS: one counts Flower Child units, the other counts North Italia restaurants — distinct brands, distinct unit populations. The only thing linking them is the shared token "count," which the suggester flagged on token overlap alone. Scope also differs (two different concept footprints). Keep separate — these are not the same metric.

Only the words "gift" and "card" overlap; the economics are opposite. (1) Different object: "gift_card_inventory_writedown" is the physical unsold gift-card stock (an asset) being impaired ("$7.9 million write-down of gift card inventory"), while "gift_card_redemptions" is customers spending their gift-card balances ("higher gift card redemptions"). (2) Different scope: a is a one-time non-recurring charge; b is an ongoing operational sales/revenue flow. (3) Different mechanism: a is an accounting write-down (a loss/cost on an asset); b is redemption activity that drives revenue recognition. All three SAME criteria fail, so keep them separate.

Token overlap (income, spending) only. Evidence shows different object, scope, and mechanism. A = the HIGH-income/top-end tier specifically: top-end consumers feeling pressure and trading DOWN into CAKE off steakhouses/expensive independents, plus $100K+ earners' job/income stability. B = the relative DISTRIBUTION and stability of spending ACROSS all income cohorts ("relative cohort distribution and spending patterns are pretty stable"). Object differs: one named tier's spend vs. cross-cohort mix stability. Scope differs: high-income subset vs. all cohorts. Mechanism differs: trade-down benefit driven by macro pressure on top earners vs. steadiness of the overall cohort spending mix. All three SAME tests fail, so keep separate.

These are opposite ends of the income spectrum, not the same driver. Same object (consumer spending) and same scope (CAKE's demand), but the MECHANISM is the opposite. (1) high_income: evidence is about wealthy/"top end" consumers with "$100K+ incomes" trading DOWN from steakhouses/expensive independents into CAKE — CAKE benefits when high-end consumers feel pressure. (2) low_end: evidence is about "low-end consumers" being cited by management — the budget-constrained segment, which pressures CAKE rather than benefiting it. They track different customer cohorts with inverse effects on the business: high-income weakness is a tailwind (trade-down), low-end weakness is a headwind. Mechanism check FAILS, so verdict is DIFFERENT. Merging would erase the directional signal (which segment is hurting and whether it helps or hurts CAKE).

Only the token "revenue" overlaps (the suggester's own weak signal: token_overlap:revenue). All three SAME tests fail. (1) Object/metric differs: "international_royalty_revenue" is a narrow revenue stream from international licensing + consumer packaged goods royalties; "revenue_surprise" is total company revenue measured against guidance ("revenue within our guidance range", "exceeded the high end of the guidance"). (2) Scope differs: a sub-segment royalty slice vs whole-company total revenues. (3) Mechanism differs: earning royalties by licensing the brand vs total revenue beating/meeting/missing the expected range. Keep separate.

Token overlap (sales/same/store) flagged these, but the scope test fails. Side A's evidence is explicitly about INTERNATIONAL comp sales — "all comp positive... in all those different geographies," "the brand continues to resonate everywhere around the world." Side B's evidence is the company's core comparable RESTAURANT sales metric, anchored to The Cheesecake Factory restaurants and domestic concepts ("Comparable restaurant sales at The Cheesecake Factory restaurants increased 4.0%," "slowing growth of our concepts domestically," "inability to grow comparable restaurant sales"). Test 1 (metric): both are same-store/comparable sales — pass. Test 2 (scope): FAIL — international geographies/foreign markets vs. the overall (domestic-anchored) company comparable-restaurant-sales metric; the "international_" qualifier marks a deliberate geographic sub-scope. Test 3 (mechanism): differs — international brand resonance in foreign markets vs. core US restaurant traffic/pricing. This is a child/parent (international subset vs. overall), not the same object. Keep separate; an optional related-link is fine but NOT a SAME_AS merge.

Token overlap on "marketing" only. Fails all three tests. (1) Object/metric differs: marketing_costs is a spending/expense line item (Cheesecake Rewards launch costs, marketing spend driving operating expenses up); social_media_marketing's lone quote is about competitors' social media use and differentiating social media messaging — a competitive risk factor, not a cost. (2) Scope differs: total marketing spend vs. the narrow social-media channel. (3) Mechanism differs: marketing_costs raises expenses and pressures margins; the social_media_marketing quote is a competitive-differentiation risk (rivals improving social media makes it harder to stand out), not a cost mechanism. Keep separate.

Same object (net income margin) and same scope (CAKE company-level fiscal/quarterly margin), but DIFFERENT mechanism. Side A is forward-looking guidance: every quote is "we expect / we anticipate / we would anticipate net income margin of approximately X%" — a projection the company issues. Side B is the surprise/outcome: every quote is a realized result measured against the prior guide — "of 3.5% was at the high end of the range", "exceeded the high end of the guidance", "exceeded the 3.5% guidance we provided". Guidance (the forecast) vs surprise (actual-vs-forecast beat/miss) are distinct driver mechanisms — one is the issued forecast, the other is the realization gap. Mechanism test fails, so they must stay separate. (Note: side A's same_as_variant net_margin_guidance and side B's profit_margin_surprise reinforce that these are the guidance vs surprise families, not the same driver.)

These measure two different objects, so they fail test 1 (same metric). "new_restaurant_margin" tracks the PROFIT MARGIN of new restaurants ramping toward mature margins ("The mature margin is one thing, but if you can bring the early stages of the restaurants up faster"). "new_restaurant_unit_volumes" tracks SALES VOLUME / revenue per unit ("practically at the same unit volumes as the rest of the North system"). Margin (profitability %) and unit volume (sales dollars per store) are distinct metrics with distinct mechanisms — the second quote even separates them explicitly ("at the same unit volumes... AND their margins are great"), treating volume and margin as two different things. Token overlap on "new/restaurant" only reflects that both concern new-store ramp-up, not the same measured quantity. Keep separate.

These share tokens but mean opposite things. (1) Object/metric differs: A = sales GENERATED BY new restaurants ("great sales performance out of the new locations"); B = sales SHIFTED AWAY from existing restaurants by new ones ("pressure from some sales transfer from recently opened restaurants") — i.e., cannibalization, confirmed by B's own variant new_unit_cannibalization. (2) Scope differs: A measures the new units' own revenue; B measures the negative impact on existing/comparable-store sales. (3) Mechanism differs: A is a growth/contribution driver (positive); B is a transfer/cannibalization headwind (negative pressure). Token overlap (new, unit, sales) is the only thing in common; the economics are inverse. Keep separate.

Different object, scope, and mechanism — only the tokens "north/italia/average" overlap. (1) OBJECT: A = "average check" = dollars one customer spends per visit (~$34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner). B = "average unit volume / AUV" = total annual sales of one restaurant (~$8.0M / $7.7M). Tens of dollars vs millions of dollars = not the same metric. (2) SCOPE: A is per-customer, per-single-transaction; B is per-restaurant-location, per-full-year. (3) MECHANISM: A is driven by menu price x items per order; B is driven by check size x transaction count x seats x operating days (AUV is a function that includes average check, not equal to it). Word "average" modifies different nouns in each. All three tests fail → keep separate.

Token overlap is only the brand name "North Italia"; the metrics are fundamentally different. A = average unit volume (sales DOLLARS per restaurant: "$8.0 million", "annualized AUVs of $7.7 million"). B = restaurant COUNT ("North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants", fiscal.ai kpi north_italia_company_owned_restaurants). Same brand scope, but FAIL test 1 (different object: dollars-per-unit vs number-of-units) and FAIL test 3 (different mechanism: revenue intensity vs unit count). Keep separate.

These are two distinct restaurant metrics for the same brand (North Italia), but they measure different objects. Fails test #1 (same object/metric).  Side A = Average Unit Volume (AUV): a DOLLAR LEVEL metric. Evidence: "Average sales per mature location... were approximately $8.0 million for fiscal 2022" and "annualized AUVs of $7.7 million." It answers "how much does one restaurant sell per year?" (a per-store revenue level, e.g. $7.7M-$8.0M).  Side B = Same-Store / Comparable Sales: a PERCENT-CHANGE metric. Evidence (20+ refs): "comparable sales increased approximately 8% from fiscal 2022", "Comparable restaurant sales vs. prior year 9%", "comparable sales decreased approximately 2%". It answers "how fast are existing-store sales growing/shrinking year-over-year?" (a growth rate, e.g. +8%, -2%).  Object/metric differs: dollar level per unit (AUV) vs. year-over-year growth rate (comp sales). These are standard, separate restaurant-industry KPIs. A company can have a high AUV ($8M) while comp sales decline (-2%) in the same period — confirming they are not the same measurement. The token overlap ("north", "italia") only reflects the shared brand, not a shared metric. Keep separate.

Both mention "North Italia" (a CAKE restaurant brand), but they track different objects/metrics with different mechanisms. (1) Object/metric: "development" is about the company's ability to develop, grow, and operate the brand as a concept — a strategic/operational risk factor from 10-K filings. "traffic" is a specific quarterly performance metric — guest visit counts ("traffic was negative 4%/-6%") discussed in earnings transcripts alongside price and mix. These are not the same metric. (2) Scope: development covers the whole brand build-out/growth strategy; traffic is one narrow same-store sales component. (3) Mechanism: development risk flows from execution of expansion and concept operation; traffic flows from consumer demand showing up at existing stores. Token overlap on "north"/"italia" only reflects the shared brand name, not the same driver. The traffic side's variant "north_italia_restaurant_traffic" confirms it is a traffic-specific driver, distinct from development. Keep separate.

Only the brand "North Italia" overlaps (why token overlap flagged it). The metrics differ on object and mechanism: A = food cost (dollars spent on ingredients, a COGS/margin metric driven by operating efficiency); B = company-owned restaurant count (number of physical locations, a unit/footprint metric driven by expansion). Same scope (North Italia brand) but Test 1 (same object/metric) and Test 3 (same mechanism) both fail. Keep separate.

Same brand (North Italia, CAKE), but different object and mechanism. (A) food cost = the cost of ingredients/food, a single input expense line the company aims to lower. (B) restaurant-level margin (a.k.a. four-wall margin) = a profitability percentage after ALL store-level costs (food, labor, occupancy), driven here by menu price increases. Food cost is just one input to margin; lowering food cost can raise margin, but they are not the same metric, scope, or mechanism. Evidence confirms: A's quote is about deploying best practices to "lower food costs"; B's quotes report "restaurant-level margin... 15%" / "four-wall margin... 13.3%... supported by a menu price increase." Object fails (cost line vs margin %) and mechanism fails (cost reduction vs pricing/total-cost leverage). Keep separate.

Same brand (North Italia, CAKE) but different metric and mechanism. (a) "the north Italia mix was negative 2%" is a sales/comp mix percentage that moves with menu/pricing/traffic. (b) "North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants" is a count of restaurant locations that moves with unit openings/closings. Object/metric differs (percent mix vs. location count) and mechanism differs, so SAME tests 1 and 3 both fail. Token overlap on "north"+"italia" is just the shared brand name, exactly the suggester's flagged reason. Keep separate.

Different object/metric and different mechanism, so they fail the SAME test. Side A (north_italia_menu_pricing) is about the price increases North Italia takes on its menu — the quotes discuss timing of a price increase ("another price increase in October") and whether they were "behind on pricing." That is a pricing-action driver (an input/lever the company pulls). Side B (north_italia_restaurant_level_margin) is about the restaurant-level / four-wall profit margin — the quotes report margin figures ("margin... improved to 13.3%", "restaurant-level margin... was 15%, a 250 basis point improvement"). That is a profitability OUTCOME metric, not a pricing action. Although a menu price increase can SUPPORT margin (side B's Feb-2023 quote even says margin was "supported by a menu price increase"), that is a cause-and-effect relationship between two distinct things, not the same thing. The object differs (price level vs. profit margin %), and the mechanism differs (raising menu prices vs. measuring four-wall profitability). Scope overlaps (both North Italia, CAKE), but rules 1 and 3 fail, so verdict is DIFFERENT. Note side B already carries margin-synonym variants (four_wall_margin, restaurant_margin) confirming it is the margin cluster, distinct from a pricing driver.

These are two different metrics that merely share the words "north italia restaurant."  Side A (north_italia_restaurant_count): counts physical locations. Evidence quote: "North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants" — a unit count of restaurants.  Side B (north_italia_restaurant_level_margin): a profitability percentage. Evidence quotes: "restaurant-level margin for the adjusted mature North Italia locations was 15%" and "Forewall margin... improved to 13.3%" — a profit margin (% of sales).  Three-part test: 1. Same object/metric? NO — a count of stores (a number of units) vs a margin (a profitability ratio). Completely different quantities and units (stores vs %). 2. Same scope? Both about North Italia, but A is the whole company-owned restaurant base while B is specifically "adjusted mature North Italia locations." Different. 3. Same mechanism? NO — counting outlets vs measuring per-restaurant profit.  Token overlap (italia, north, restaurant) is coincidental brand+segment naming. A store count and a margin are never the same driver. Keep them separate.

Token overlap reflects only the shared brand name "North Italia," not a shared metric. Object differs: a = count of company-owned restaurant locations (unit/store count); b = comparable/same-store sales growth (a revenue-change percentage). Mechanism differs: a = footprint/expansion (number of open restaurants); b = year-over-year demand/traffic/pricing at existing stores. These are distinct KPIs — restaurant count can rise while same-store sales fall. Fails test 1 (same object/metric) and test 3 (same mechanism). Keep separate.

Token overlap is only the brand name ("North Italia"), not the metric. The metrics are different objects: A is restaurant-level / four-wall MARGIN, a profitability ratio (evidence: "restaurant-level margin... was 15%", "Four-wall margin... improved to 13.3%"). B is same-store / COMPARABLE SALES, a revenue-growth measure (evidence: "comparable sales increased approximately 8%", "Comparable restaurant sales vs. prior year 9%"). Scope matches (both North Italia brand), but object/metric differs (profit % vs sales growth %) and mechanism differs (cost/profitability efficiency vs sales trend vs prior year). Two of the three required conditions fail, so keep them separate.

Same metric type and same mechanism, but DIFFERENT SCOPE — so the SAME test fails on rule 2 (same scope).  Metric (rule 1): both measure restaurant-level / four-wall margin (a profitability % per restaurant). PASS. Mechanism (rule 3): both driven by menu price increases, sales leverage, inflation/cost control. PASS. Scope (rule 2): FAILS. The two drivers track different brand/concept units within CAKE:  - side a "north_italia_restaurant_level_margin" = margin for ONLY the North Italia concept. Every quote names North Italia explicitly: "margin for the adjusted mature North Italia locations improved to 13.3%", "restaurant-level margin for the adjusted mature North Italia locations was 15%". Its variants (north_italia_four_wall_margin, north_italia_margin, north_italia_restaurant_margin) all confirm North-Italia-only scope.  - side b "restaurant_level_margin" = the company-wide / Cheesecake-Factory-concept margin. Quotes repeatedly tie it to "The Cheesecake Factory concept" ("increase restaurant-level margins at The Cheesecake Factory concept", "Cheesecake Factory restaurant level margins for the quarter improved... 18.4%") plus portfolio-level objectives in the 10-Ks ("longer-term restaurant-level margin objectives", "drive incremental sales while maintaining our restaurant level margins").  CAKE operates multiple concepts (Cheesecake Factory, North Italia, Flower Child/FRC). North Italia margins (~13-15%) and Cheesecake Factory margins (~16-18.5%) are reported as distinct figures in the same calls. Merging them would collapse a concept-specific driver into a different concept's / company-wide driver — exactly the kind of over-merge to avoid. Note side b's variants do NOT include any North-Italia name, reinforcing they were kept separate intentionally.  Verdict: keep separate (DIFFERENT). The token overlap (level, margin, restaurant) is real but the brand qualifier "north_italia" marks a genuinely narrower scope.

Token overlap (sales/same/store) only — the meaning fails all three tests.  1. SAME OBJECT? NO. A is North Italia's comparable-sales metric (one specific restaurant brand). B is company-wide same-store-sales guidance, with no scoping to North Italia at all. Different object.  2. SAME SCOPE? NO. A = single brand (North Italia) within The Cheesecake Factory portfolio. B = consolidated/company-level comp. Brand-specific vs whole-company.  3. SAME MECHANISM? NO — and this is the decisive split. A is REPORTED ACTUALS: "comparable sales increased approximately 8% from fiscal 2022" (10-K/10-Q/8-K backward-looking results). B is FORWARD GUIDANCE / forward expectation: "do you expect to be in that 1% to 2% range on comp in the first quarter?", "implied 2026 same-shore sales number?", "our comp assumptions", "the high end of our guide." Actual-results metric vs guidance is a different mechanism.  Merging these would repeat the v2-style over-merge error (collapsing a specific brand metric and a generic forward-guidance driver because words overlap). Keep separate.

Token overlap on "off,premise" is surface-level only. The two refer to different objects/metrics with different mechanisms.  off_premise_sales = the off-premise revenue CHANNEL / sales volume itself. Evidence is overwhelmingly about the size of that channel as a metric: "off-premise sales totaled 22% of sales", "Sales through the off-premise channel comprised approximately 21% of our restaurant sales". This is an outcome metric (a revenue stream / channel mix). Its accepted variants (off_premise_channel_mix, off_premise_sales_mix) confirm the object = the channel's share of sales.  off_premise_promotions = a MARKETING ACTION/lever applied to that channel, not the channel's sales. Its single evidence quote: "we do the marketing and that type of offer is really off-premise driven. And we do that on occasion." The object here is a promotional offer/marketing tactic, an INPUT that could influence sales, not the sales metric itself.  Test results: 1. Same object/metric? NO — one is the sales channel/its share (an output metric); the other is a marketing promotion (an input lever). 2. Same scope? Partially overlapping channel, but different facet (volume of channel vs. a promo tactic targeting it). 3. Same mechanism? NO — measuring channel revenue share vs. running a marketing offer are different mechanisms.  Because object and mechanism both differ, these are distinct drivers. A promotion is a cause/lever; sales-channel mix is an effect/metric. Merging would collapse a marketing-action driver into a revenue-channel metric — wrong direction. Keep separate.

Same underlying metric (comparable/same-store restaurant sales), but scope and mechanism differ. Side A's evidence is exclusively ACTUAL/REALIZED results and risk factors: "Revenues increased 9.1%... due to an increase in comparable restaurant sales," "Comparable restaurant sales increased 4.0% year-over-year," plus a KPI line and "inability to grow comparable restaurant sales could materially adversely affect." Side B's evidence is exclusively FORWARD-LOOKING management guidance: "Our comp assumptions remain consistent," "do you expect to be in that 1% to 2% range on comp," "implied 2026 same-shore sales number," "wasn't at the high end of our guide." So the object is shared but: (2) scope fails — backward realized performance vs forward projected expectation; (3) mechanism fails — reporting achieved sales vs management forecast/assumption. A guidance/forecast driver moves the stock differently from the actual reported result (surprise vs realization), so they must stay separate. Keep distinct.
